ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'chicken rice dish' class; no specific entry for black pepper spiced Malay-style chicken rice. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP covers chicken-and-rice mixed dishes broadly; no specific black pepper variant code exists. Closest parent match used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 450 g, comprising a portion of black pepper-seasoned chicken, steamed white rice, and accompanying side vegetables such as tomato and cucumber.

nutrient_source.title: Singapore Food Composition Database — Black Pepper Chicken Rice
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat (1.78 g/100 g), trans fat (0.04 g/100 g), and vitamin C (2.22 mg/100 g) are not available in the HPB database for this dish; these values are retained from the input record's per_100g_meta which attributes them to a secondary nutritional panel source at confidence 0.70, and are flagged as indicative pending confirmation from an approved primary source. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water are not reported by either source and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Black Pepper Soul Fire Chicken Rice as a composite dish. GI of 72 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate component: white jasmine rice (cooked), which has a well-established GI of approximately 72 (high) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 68.04) / 100 ≈ 49.0, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 15.12 × 4.5 = 68.04 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 324 mg/100 g with insufficient potassium (182 mg/100 g) and fibre (1.0 g/100 g) offset
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 324 mg/100 g is in the borderline range (200–400 mg); potassium at 182 mg/100 g falls below the 200 mg/100 g threshold needed to offset borderline sodium, and dietary fibre at 1.0 g/100 g is well below 3 g/100 g — individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium soy sauce, avoid adding condiments at the table, and increase vegetable portions to improve the sodium-to-potassium ratio.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI ≈ 72) with low dietary fibre (1.0 g/100 g) producing a high glycemic load per serving (~49)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The white rice base carries a GI of approximately 72 and delivers approximately 68 g of carbohydrate per standard 450 g serving, generating a glycemic load of ~49 — individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice to reduce GI and glycemic load, increase vegetable portions to add fibre, and retain black pepper which has evidence-based benefits for glucose metabolism via piperine.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Borderline sodium (324 mg/100 g), moderate saturated fat (1.78 g/100 g), and low trans fat (0.04 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat at 1.78 g/100 g and trans fat at 0.04 g/100 g are relatively low, but sodium at 324 mg/100 g contributes meaningfully to daily sodium load at a 450 g serving (~1458 mg sodium per plate, approximately 63% of the 2300 mg daily limit) — individuals with cardiovascular disease should request reduced-sodium preparation, limit portion size, and pair with fibre-rich vegetables to support lipid and blood pressure management.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory black pepper (piperine), low arachidonic acid from white chicken meat, low saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Black pepper (piperine) provides evidence-based anti-inflammatory benefit; saturated fat is low at 1.78 g/100 g; however, nightshade-sensitive RA patients should request omission of tomato, and all RA patients should request reduced-sodium preparation and consider brown rice substitution to reduce systemic glycemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Black Pepper Soul Fire Chicken Rice is a Singaporean Malay-style rice dish featuring grilled or pan-seared chicken pieces coated in a bold, peppery black pepper sauce served alongside steamed white jasmine rice and fresh side vegetables such as tomato and cucumber. The dish is characterised by its intensely spiced, slightly smoky black pepper marinade — a hallmark of the 'soul fire' preparation style — which combines coarsely ground black pepper, soy sauce, garlic, and onion to create a savoury, mildly fiery flavour profile with a tender, juicy chicken texture. Per 100 g, the dish provides 116 kcal, 4.96 g protein, 4.0 g fat (1.78 g saturated), 15.12 g carbohydrate, 1.56 g sugar, 1.0 g dietary fibre, and 324 mg sodium. The standard serving size is approximately 450 g (one plate). Clinically, the dish is high-GI due to its white rice base (GI ≈ 72, GL ≈ 49 per serving), making it unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes without rice substitution. Sodium at 324 mg/100 g is borderline for hypertension management. Saturated fat is moderate and trans fat is negligible. Black pepper (piperine) contributes anti-inflammatory and glucose-metabolism benefits. The dish is halal-certified in most Singaporean hawker and food court contexts and is a popular lunch and dinner option across Singapore's Malay food stalls and fast-casual dining outlets.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C supplemented from secondary nutritional panel data (confidence 0.70) as the best available data pending an approved primary source confirmation. GI estimated from the dominant white rice component using the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source. Overall record confidence is high given the HPB primary source coverage of all core macros.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation and moisture data for this specific black pepper chicken rice preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database as of 2026-04-17. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, and selenium_mcg are set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database for this dish as of 2026-04-17. Saturated fat (1.78 g/100 g), trans fat (0.04 g/100 g), and vitamin C (2.22 mg/100 g) are sourced from secondary nutritional panel data at confidence 0.70 and should be treated as indicative pending confirmation from an approved primary source such as HPB or USDA FoodData Central. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white jasmine rice (cooked) from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283); no direct GI measurement for this composite dish exists in the approved GI literature. Glycemic load per 450 g serving calculated as (72 × 68.04) / 100 ≈ 49.0. The dish is classified as halal based on standard Malay hawker preparation; individual vendor halal certification should be verified. No zero-value core macros are present in this record.
